excerpt: None can unlock the inner meanings (taʾwīl) of the Book save Allah Himself and those He has established in knowledge—the Imams.
source_authority: The foundational Ismaili work on Qur'anic interpretation
source_location_hint: Exegesis on Q. 3:7

excerpt: The Qur'an and the Ahl al-Bayt shall never be separated from one another until they meet the Prophet at the Pool of Kausar, signifying that in every age there must exist an Imam to expound the teachings.
source_authority: The authoritative collection of the Chief Judge's sermons
source_location_hint: Tradition of the Twin Legacies (al-Thaqalayn)

excerpt: In the verse of inheritance of the Book, those foremost in good deeds are the Imams of every era; those moderate recognize the Imam's rights; those who wrong themselves doubt or distance from the Imams.
source_location_hint: Q. 35:32 commentary

excerpt: Allah has sustained Prophethood and Imamate through the descendants of Abraham, and from their progeny raised up Imams whose obedience is obedience to Allah.
source_authority: Premier philosophical defense of Ismaili theology
source_location_hint: On divine bestowal of the kingdom upon the Ahl al-Bayt

excerpt: The Qur'an designates as the family of a people their very descendants through blood kinship (nasab), as shown in the verse of the believer from Pharaoh's household.
source_location_hint: Exegesis on Q. 40:28

excerpt: Those who follow and obey the Ahl al-Bayt in matters of faith shall be counted among them, as exemplified in the verses concerning the families of the prophets and Pharaoh.
source_authority: Pinnacle of medieval Ismaili philosophical synthesis
source_location_hint: On spiritual affiliation and the meaning of 'family' (āl)

excerpt: Allah answered his prayer and brought forth from his descendants a chain of divinely guided leaders, beginning with the Messenger of Allah and continuing through Maulana Ali and his progeny.
source_location_hint: theological exposition on the prophetic line and Imamate

excerpt: Only the Imams possess the claim to be those whom idolatry has never touched, whose hearts have never inclined toward prostration before any save Allah.
source_location_hint: on the purity and spiritual rank of the Imams

excerpt: Allah then multiplied his line and established prophethood and imamate among his descendants.
source_location_hint: on the covenant with Abraham and the continuation of Imamate

excerpt: Allah declares His will to keep them perpetually free from filth. The Messenger of Allah declares: 'My lineage has ever moved from pure wombs to pure loins, and no filth has ever touched us.'
source_location_hint: on the purification of the Ahl al-Bayt and the immaculate lineage

excerpt: The light traveled downward through successive pure loins and pure wombs until it reached the loins of Abraham, the Friend of Allah, and never did filth and impurity of idolatry touch this light.
source_location_hint: on the prophetic and Imamate light transmitted through pure generations

excerpt: Allah has granted the exclusive privilege to the Prophets and Imams that they cannot be born from the wombs of idolaters; yet they may produce disbelievers, as Noah had a disbelieving son.
source_location_hint: on the nature of prophetic and Imamate descent and the exception of rebellious offspring

excerpt: Those who have advanced in spiritual understanding and joined themselves to the Imam become united with the divine and human dimensions; all other things devoid of this connection possess no true value.
source_location_hint: esoteric exegesis on the station of the Imam and the significance of the verse 'All things have been preserved in a Clear Book'

excerpt: The necessity of the Imam is essential in natural disposition, innate constitution, nature, intellect, governance, prescription, and norms. It is obligatory in all revealed laws, religions, and religious communities, just as the existence of a Creator is necessary.
source_work: Kitab al-Ithbat al-Imamate (The Affirmation of the Imamate)
source_location_hint: Opening exposition on necessity of Imamate

excerpt: If people's struggle in seeking what the soul requires matched their struggle for bodily needs, none would doubt, stray, or ascribe human qualities to Allah—neither through anthropomorphism nor polytheistic association.
source_location_hint: Discussion of symbolized realities and degrees of excellence

excerpt: All creation and its needs admonish regarding the symbolized religious reality. Such creation serves as witness for whoever seeks testimony and proof for whoever asks—yet it is also proof against those who deny the symbolized religious realities of the Imams and their hierarchical ranks.
source_location_hint: Section on natural and legal testimony

excerpt: True judgment in religion rests upon two witnesses: one established by religious law and the other compelled by nature. The horizons and inner selves remind, enforce, and verify the conventional witness.
source_location_hint: Exposition of double testimony—legal and natural

excerpt: The essence is the ultimate upon which all others rest and excels over all others, since the remaining nine categories are established from it. This essence serves as proof for the permanent, intellectual, luminous, authentic substance through which all souls are illumined—that is the Imam.
source_location_hint: Discussion of the ten categories

excerpt: Among the four constituents, heat is active and noblest, from which arise luminosity, light, motion, and elevation. Likewise, the Imam illumines the soul; from Him comes intellectual light, motion, and elevation. No principle or religious realm is complete without him.
source_location_hint: Analogy of the four elements and their summits

excerpt: The outermost celestial sphere in which all orbit is the supreme sphere; likewise the Imam is the supreme religious sphere for all other spheres. Fire is the noblest element; likewise the Imam is the highest religious pillar illuminating souls.
source_location_hint: Section on celestial spheres and elements

excerpt: The sun is the peak and summit in nobility among planets, through which all plants, animals, and newborns grow and achieve perfection. Likewise, the Imam is the sun of religion through which souls are illumined by guidance and wisdom.
source_location_hint: Analogy of the Sun as religious illumination

excerpt: Among the twelve zodiacal signs, the fourth is the peak in nobility; similarly, among the twelve proofs, the one directly connected to the Imam is the most excellent and exalted of all.
source_location_hint: Discussion of zodiacal signs and their spiritual correspondence

excerpt: The fourth clime, parallel to the fourth heaven and to the sun, is the most moderate and most excellent of all. Similarly, the Imam is the most excellent in the hierarchy, the peak and apex of all.
source_location_hint: Analogy of the fourth clime and its perfection

excerpt: Gold among metals is the most excellent, with which commerce is conducted; likewise the Imam is the most excellent religious and intellectual substance through whom worldly and religious transactions are completed. The red ruby is the crown among jewels; thus the Imam is the peak among creation.
source_location_hint: Discourse on minerals and precious stones as proofs for the Imam

excerpt: Among the seven grains, wheat is the peak and summit, most balanced in nature and nobility. Likewise, the Imam is the peak and summit, benefiting and nourishing souls with his knowledge, source of their spiritual livelihood.
source_location_hint: Analogy of grains and their relationship to spiritual sustenance

excerpt: Among fruit-bearing trees, the date palm is the peak; everything in it is beneficial and nothing wasted. The Imam is the head and leader of all believers, symbolized by the date palm's abundance and utility.
source_location_hint: Analogy of the date palm and its comprehensive benefit

excerpt: Medicinal plants may become antidotes for those on the verge of death; likewise the Imam's words are antidotes for souls, healing diseases, afflictions, doubts, and discords. The fatal poisons represent opponents who usurp the Imam's position.
source_location_hint: Analogy of medicinal plants and the Imam's spiritual healing

excerpt: The horse is the best among animals, the eagle among birds, and the lion among beasts of prey. Their disparities in strength and training point to a necessary peak within the human species—the Imam.
source_location_hint: Analogy of animals as evidence for human spiritual hierarchy

excerpt: Sweet, pleasant plants symbolize the Imams, symbols of their speech and knowledge that entice the soul and please the eye. The bitter, thorny plants symbolize the leaders of the literalists.
source_location_hint: Discussion of plants and their spiritual correspondence; citation of Quran 14:24-26

excerpt: Allah clarified that the earth can never be deprived of an Imam, even if all people were impeccable and saintly. The appointment of the Imam is not by choice or consultation, but by divine designation.
source_location_hint: Exegesis of Quran 2:30 (Adam's appointment) as proof of necessary Imamate

excerpt: Just as the testimony of the horizons and inner selves is compulsory and not voluntary, making it impossible to alter or substitute, the Messenger made testimony compulsory regarding the Imamate.
source_location_hint: Connecting mandatory nature testimony with mandatory religious testimony

excerpt: The sunna (way) of the Imamate itself, not the sunna of ritual practices, remained unchanged. The Prophet changed the sunna of all previous prophets regarding worship but not regarding the Imamate.
source_location_hint: Discussion of prophetic precedent and continuity of Imamate across cycles

excerpt: Noah appointed his son to the wilāya (guardianship) and Imamate by designation and appointment, and it continued thereafter. The ark was the Imam himself; Noah's invitation was to the Imam.
source_location_hint: Noah's cycle as proof of continuous Imamate by designation

excerpt: Abraham and Ishmael built the House (Kaʿba), commanding people to glorify it—an indication that the Imamate would remain in Abraham's house and progeny, urging veneration of his family's Imams.
source_location_hint: Abraham's covenant and the Imamate in his progeny

excerpt: The Prophet ordered people to turn toward the Kaʿba (built by Abraham and Ishmael), indicating the Imamate's return to Abraham's house—the family of Ishmael, the children of the Prophet Muhammad.
source_location_hint: Change of qibla as proof of Imamate's continuation in Muhammad's family

excerpt: The virtues by which Allah has preferred some people consist of three kinds: knowledge, struggle in Allah's cause, and piety. The Commander of the Faithful had precedence in all these virtues.
source_location_hint: Qur'anic basis for the three virtues establishing Imamate

excerpt: The wars of the Commander of the Faithful ʿAlī are well-known and famous. Piety comes through knowledge—knowing what belongs to one and what belongs to others; thus the Imamate belonged to the Commander of the Faithful.
source_location_hint: ʿAlī's virtues as necessary qualifications for Imamate

excerpt: The Prophet mentioned his rank and station without humiliation. He said 'I am the master of the Arabs without boasting' and 'I am the most eloquent without boasting.' The Commander of the Faithful said: 'Ask me before you lose me.'
source_location_hint: Prophetic and Imam's acknowledgment of their station as proper humility

excerpt: When ʿUmar ascended the pulpit after Abū Bakr, he said: 'The oath to Abū Bakr was a hasty decision, but Allah has averted its evil from us.' This expounds the falsehood of claims that Abū Bakr's leadership was indicated by designation.
source_location_hint: ʿUmar's own testimony against Abū Bakr's designation

excerpt: The merits of the lord of the age and time (al-Ḥākim) exceed what can be mentioned; yet one may speak according to limited knowledge. Allah said 'They have not valued Allah according to His true value.' The Commander of the Faithful is the most worthy to accept his servant's excuse.
source_location_hint: Introduction to discussion of the current Imam's virtues and merits

excerpt: The outer earth shines through the justice of the Commander of the Faithful, and the inner earth shines through knowledge and explanation. Allah made the earth radiant with His light.
source_location_hint: The Commander of the Faithful as manifestation of divine light

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful is the peak of all peaks, the ultimate limit beyond which there is no other. Through him all affairs of prophets, trustees, and Imams became complete; their order was perfected and perfect wisdom manifested in their disposition, laws, and interpretations.
source_location_hint: Al-Ḥākim as the perfector of all predecessors' missions

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful is like the heart and all others like limbs. Through the heart all limbs receive life and recompense. It is through the Commander of the Faithful that people gain recompense and acceptance of life.
source_location_hint: The Imam as the spiritual heart of creation and humanity

excerpt: Just as humankind accepted all from previous realms and exceeds them in things they did not possess, so the Commander of the Faithful gathers all merits of those before him, their knowledge and benefit, exceeding them many times over.
source_location_hint: The Imam as the quintessence of creation and its perfection

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful gathered merits of all prophets, Imams, and sages who preceded him, exceeding them in things of which they were incapable. He is the sum of everything in the worlds—spiritual, physical, and conventional. All are his agents until the Day of Judgment.
source_location_hint: Universal comprehensiveness of the Imam's wisdom and authority

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful is the Qā'im in his time and the Qā'im of all who preceded him, the perfector of them all. He is the sixth through whom the seventh's command is perfected. By another calculation he is the ninth, and the perfection of nine months completes the human creature.
source_location_hint: The Imam's role in multiple eschatological and cosmic calculations

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful is the second through whom the order at the beginning of each cycle is perfected. He is the sixth through whose sword Allah will conquer the whole world, just as through the Prophet, the sixth from Adam.
source_location_hint: The Imam's cosmic role in cycles of prophecy and creation

excerpt: The Commander of the Faithful arose perfecting the vicegerency of the Creator among His creatures, dealing with creation as Allah deals with His creatures regarding their constitution and sustenance. The Prophet said: 'The ruler is the shadow of Allah on the earth'—meaning the Commander of the Faithful.
source_location_hint: The Imam as Allah's perfect shadow and vicegerent

excerpt: Philosophy is seeking to resemble Allah according to human capacity—dealing with mankind as Allah deals with them. The philosophers stole this speech from the people of truth, though none but the Commander of the Faithful has carried out its true meaning in all his treatment of humankind.
source_location_hint: The Imam as the true practitioner of divine philosophy

excerpt: Allah bestows blessings on all people—pious or impious, believers or unbelievers. If He gave only to believers, faith would be only for worldly gain; if He denied all to believers, they would despair. Thus He gives to some and denies to others at different times.
source_location_hint: Divine distributive justice as model for the Imam's governance

excerpt: Allah gave worldly goods to the pious and impious alike, so that the deeds of the pious would not be for the world, nor restraint from immorality only for it. The Commander of the Faithful gives worldly benefits to the righteous and immoral alike.
source_location_hint: The Imam follows divine pattern in temporal distribution

excerpt: Allah has concealed the means to acquire worldly benefits; one becomes rich through one means while another through the same means has nothing. If all knew one means of obtaining worldly goods from the Commander of the Faithful, all other means would become futile.
source_location_hint: Divine concealment of means as wisdom reflected in the Imam's governance
